card.title: You Are Mostly Microbes
card.teaser: You carry around 38 trillion bacteria — and without them, you couldn't digest breakfast or fight off a cold.
card.coverImagePrompt: A translucent human silhouette filled with swirling, glowing bacterial colonies in greens and pinks, microscopic texture layered over anatomical form, cinematic scientific illustration

lessons.title: You are a walking ecosystem
lessons.body: If you counted every cell in your body, only about half would be human. The rest — roughly 38 trillion of them — are bacteria, fungi, and viruses living on your skin, in your mouth, and packed tightest of all in your gut. By cell count, you are more microbe than person.

visual.imagePrompt: A human body outline glowing with faint swirling dots representing microbes, silhouette art, ethereal

lessons.title: Meet the microbiome
lessons.body: The 'microbiome' is the collective community of microorganisms living in and on you. Your gut alone hosts over 1,000 different bacterial species, each with its own specialty — breaking down fiber, making vitamins, or training your immune cells. Together they carry about 150 times more genes than your own DNA.

keyTerms.definition: The full community of microbes living in a specific environment, like your gut
A microbe that lives with a host without harming it — often helping

visual.imagePrompt: A colorful pie chart of diverse bacterial species inside a stylized gut cross-section, scientific illustration

lessons.title: What they actually do for you
lessons.body: Your gut microbes digest fibers your own enzymes can't touch, producing short-chain fatty acids that feed your intestinal cells. They synthesize vitamin K, several B vitamins, and chemicals that help regulate your mood. They also crowd out harmful invaders — simply by taking up the space and nutrients pathogens would need to gain a foothold.

visual.imagePrompt: Stylized intestinal wall with bacteria breaking down plant fibers into molecules, vibrant diagram, annotated

lessons.title: Change the microbes, change the body
lessons.body: In 2013, researchers transplanted gut bacteria from obese humans into germ-free mice — and the mice gained fat. Bacteria from lean donors kept them lean. Fecal transplants are now a standard cure for stubborn C. difficile infections, with success rates above 90 percent. Your microbes aren't just passengers — they're co-drivers.
lessons.funFact: About 70 percent of your immune cells live in the lining of your gut, in constant conversation with your microbes.

visual.imagePrompt: A scientist holding a test tube with glowing bacterial culture beside two contrasting mice silhouettes, lab setting

lessons.title: You're never really alone
lessons.body: The old idea of the body as a sterile machine is gone. You are a superorganism — a negotiation between your cells and trillions of partners you inherited at birth, picked up from food, and shaped with every meal. Taking care of you means taking care of them.

quiz.question: Roughly how many microbial cells does an average human body host?
quiz.explanation: Current estimates put the number near 38 trillion — roughly equal to the number of your own human cells.

quiz.question: Which of these is NOT a known job of your gut microbiome?
quiz.explanation: Blood circulation is the heart's job. The microbiome handles digestion, immunity, and vitamin synthesis.

quiz.question: Fecal microbiota transplants are most famously used to treat what?
quiz.explanation: FMT restores a healthy gut community and clears stubborn C. difficile infections in over 90 percent of cases.

sources.title: Revised Estimates for the Number of Human and Bacteria Cells in the Body
Gut microbiota from twins discordant for obesity modulate metabolism in mice
